turns.student: What brings you in today?
turns.patient: I've been having worsening abdominal pain and bloody stools for the past 3 weeks. It's been getting to the point where I'm worried about it.

turns.student: Can you tell me more about what you've been feeling?
turns.patient: The crampy lower abdominal pain is mostly on my left side, and it feels like a sharp stabbing sensation that comes in waves. It's not constant, but when it hits, it's really uncomfortable. And yeah, the bloody stools have been getting more frequent too - they're bright red blood mixed with stool, and there's mucus with them as well.

turns.student: When you have those episodes, do you notice anything else happening to your body?
turns.patient: You know, it feels like I'm always running to the bathroom. I've been having 8-10 bowel movements per day, which is way more than usual. They're loose and watery, and I feel a sense of urgency that's really uncomfortable. Sometimes I even get up at night because of it – 2 or 3 times a week, I'll wake up in the middle of the night to have a bowel movement. It's been really disrupting my sleep.

turns.student: Any sweating or clamminess when the symptoms hit?
turns.patient: I do get pretty sweaty during those episodes, but it's not just that - I also feel really fatigued and weak after I've had a bowel movement. The diarrhea has been affecting my work performance too, which is stressful on its own.

turns.student: So no perspiration at all during the episodes?
turns.patient: Actually, yeah, I do get pretty sweaty during those episodes. But like I said, it's not just that - I also feel really fatigued and weak after I've had a bowel movement. The diarrhea has been affecting my work performance too, which is stressful on its own.

turns.student: Does the discomfort stay in one spot, or does it move around?
turns.patient: The pain usually stays on my left side, but sometimes it radiates to my lower back and hips. It feels like a sharp stabbing sensation that comes in waves, so it can be hard to pinpoint exactly where it is.

turns.student: Does it radiate anywhere — like your arms, neck, or jaw?
turns.patient: The pain usually stays on my left side, but sometimes it radiates to my lower back and hips. It feels like a sharp stabbing sensation that comes in waves, so it can be hard to pinpoint exactly where it is.

turns.student: Any tingling or numbness in your arms when it happens?
turns.patient: No, I don't get any tingling or numbness in my arms. The pain and discomfort are mostly localized to my lower abdomen, left side, and lower back.

turns.student: What about your extremities — any tingling, pain, or unusual sensations?
turns.patient: No, I don't experience any tingling, pain, or unusual sensations in my hands or feet. The symptoms are mostly confined to my abdominal area and lower back.

turns.student: Have you noticed any swelling anywhere? Ankles, feet, hands?
turns.patient: The pain usually stays on my left side, but sometimes it radiates to my lower back and hips. It feels like a sharp stabbing sensation that comes in waves, so it can be hard to pinpoint exactly where it is. To answer your question specifically, I do experience achy pain in both knees and ankles, no swelling or redness.

turns.student: Do your shoes feel tighter than usual, or any puffiness in your legs?
turns.patient: The achy pain in my knees and ankles has been going on for a while now. It's not just after bowel movements either - it's pretty constant. I've tried taking ibuprofen for it, but that seems to make the abdominal symptoms worse.

turns.student: Any stomach issues during the episodes? Nausea, queasiness, upset stomach?
turns.patient: The pain and discomfort are mostly localized to my lower abdomen, left side, and lower back. When I have a bowel movement, it's usually accompanied by nausea, which can be pretty intense at times. The ibuprofen for my joint pain does seem to make things worse, though - it makes the stomach issues even more uncomfortable, so I've stopped taking it.

turns.student: Do you feel sick to your stomach when the symptoms come on?
turns.patient: The nausea can be pretty intense at times. It's not constant, but when I have a bowel movement and experience abdominal pain, the nausea usually hits right after.

turns.student: Have you had any fevers, chills, or night sweats?
turns.patient: I've actually had a low-grade fever a few times per week. It's not extremely high, but I do feel like my body temperature is slightly elevated when it happens. And no, I haven't experienced any significant chills or night sweats that I can recall. The fever seems to be tied to the episodes of abdominal pain and diarrhea.

turns.student: Have you noticed any changes in your appetite or weight?
turns.patient: I have been experiencing some unintentional weight loss over the past 3 weeks, about 4 kg (9 lbs) total. I haven't really felt like eating much due to the discomfort and fatigue from the symptoms.

turns.student: Tell me about your lifestyle — diet, exercise, daily routine.
turns.patient: My lifestyle has been pretty stressful lately. I work over 60 hours a week as a software engineer at a startup, and it's been taking a toll on my eating habits. I've been relying on takeout and fast food most nights because I don't have time to cook. As for exercise, I try to fit in a run or a gym session during my lunch break when I can, but it's not as regular as I'd like.

turns.student: How active are you physically? Do you work out or walk regularly?
turns.patient: I'm trying to stay active despite the symptoms. When I do have energy, I try to fit in a run or gym session during my lunch break, but it's not regular due to my work schedule and the fatigue from the symptoms. Lately, I've been relying on takeout and fast food most nights because I don't have time to cook.

turns.student: What does a typical day of eating look like for you?
turns.patient: To be honest, my diet has been pretty irregular lately due to my work schedule. I usually grab something quick and easy in the morning, like a granola bar or yogurt, and then it's takeout or fast food most nights because I don't have time to cook. I've tried to eat more balanced meals when I can, but it's not consistent.

turns.student: Any medical problems in your family? Parents, siblings?
turns.patient: My father has type 2 diabetes, and my mother is healthy as far as I know. As for siblings, my older sister was diagnosed with Crohn's disease at age 30, which makes me a bit concerned that there might be something like that going on with me too.

turns.student: Specifically, has anyone in your family had serious diseases?
turns.patient: My father has type 2 diabetes, and my older sister was diagnosed with Crohn's disease at age 30. That's made me a bit concerned about possibly having something similar going on with me too.
